solution:
First solution:
Choose a sequence $p_1, p_2, \dots$ of primes as follows. Let $p_1$ be any prime
dividing an element of $S$. To define $p_{j+1}$ given $p_1, \dots, p_j$,
choose an integer $N_j \in S$ relatively prime to $p_1 \cdots p_j$ and let
$p_{j+1}$ be a prime divisor of $N_j$, or stop if no such $N_j$ exists.

Since $S$ is finite, the above algorithm eventually terminates in a finite
sequence $p_1, \dots, p_k$.
Let $m$ be the smallest integer such that $p_1 \cdots p_m$ has a divisor in $S$.
(By the assumption on $S$ with $n=p_1\cdots p_k$,
$m=k$ has this property, so $m$ is well-defined.)
If $m=1$, then $p_1\in S$, and we are done, so assume $m\geq 2$.
Any divisor $d$ of $p_1\cdots p_m$ in $S$ must be a multiple of $p_m$, or else
it would also be a divisor of $p_1 \cdots p_{m-1}$, contradicting the choice
of $m$. But now $\gcd(d, N_{m-1}) = p_m$, as desired.

Second solution (from \texttt{sci.math}):
Let $n$ be the smallest integer such that $\gcd(s,n) > 1$ for all $s$ in
$n$; note that $n$ obviously has no repeated prime factors.
By the condition on $S$, there exists $s \in S$ which divides $n$.

On the other hand, if $p$ is a prime divisor of $s$, then by the choice
of $n$, $n/p$ is relatively prime to some element $t$ of $S$. Since $n$ cannot
be relatively prime to $t$, $t$ is divisible by $p$, but not by any other
prime divisor of $n$ (as those primes divide $n/p$). Thus $\gcd(s,t) = p$,
as desired.

\end{itemize}

\end{document}

descriptive_long.question:
Let $intset$ be a finite set of integers, each greater than 1.  Suppose that
for each integer $indexinteger$ there is some $setmember\in intset$ such that $\gcd(setmember,indexinteger)=1$ or
$\gcd(setmember,indexinteger)=setmember$.  Show that there exist $setmember,twinmember\in intset$ such that $\gcd(setmember,twinmember)$
is prime.
descriptive_long.solution:
First solution:
Choose a sequence $primeone, primetwo, \dots$ of primes as follows. Let $primeone$ be any prime
dividing an element of $intset$. To define $primejnext$ given $primeone, \dots, primejvar$,
choose an integer $numberjvar \in intset$ relatively prime to $primeone \cdots primejvar$ and let
$primejnext$ be a prime divisor of $numberjvar$, or stop if no such $numberjvar$ exists.

Since $intset$ is finite, the above algorithm eventually terminates in a finite
sequence $primeone, \dots, primekvar$.
Let $mininteger$ be the smallest integer such that $primeone \cdots primemvar$ has a divisor in $intset$.
(By the assumption on $intset$ with $indexinteger=primeone\cdots primekvar$,
$mininteger=maxindex$ has this property, so $mininteger$ is well-defined.)
If $mininteger=1$, then $primeone\in intset$, and we are done, so assume $mininteger\geq 2$.
Any divisor $divisorelem$ of $primeone\cdots primemvar$ in $intset$ must be a multiple of $primemvar$, or else
it would also be a divisor of $primeone \cdots p_{m-1}$, contradicting the choice
of $mininteger$. But now $\gcd(divisorelem, numbermprev) = primemvar$, as desired.

Second solution (from \texttt{sci.math}):
Let $indexinteger$ be the smallest integer such that $\gcd(setmember,indexinteger) > 1$ for all $setmember$ in
$indexinteger$; note that $indexinteger$ obviously has no repeated prime factors.
By the condition on $intset$, there exists $setmember \in intset$ which divides $indexinteger$.

On the other hand, if $primegeneric$ is a prime divisor of $setmember$, then by the choice
of $indexinteger$, $indexinteger/primegeneric$ is relatively prime to some element $twinmember$ of $intset$. Since $indexinteger$ cannot
be relatively prime to $twinmember$, $twinmember$ is divisible by $primegeneric$, but not by any other
prime divisor of $indexinteger$ (as those primes divide $indexinteger/primegeneric$). Thus $\gcd(setmember,twinmember) = primegeneric$,
as desired.

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
First solution:
Choose a sequence $ornamenta, chandelier, \dots$ of primes as follows. Let $ornamenta$ be any prime
dividing an element of $lighthouse$. To define $aftershocknext$ given $ornamenta, \dots, aftershock$,
choose an integer $roundabout \in lighthouse$ relatively prime to $ornamenta \cdots aftershock$ and let
$aftershocknext$ be a prime divisor of $roundabout$, or stop if no such $roundabout$ exists.

Since $lighthouse$ is finite, the above algorithm eventually terminates in a finite
sequence $ornamenta, \dots, blacksmith$.
Let $dragonfly$ be the smallest integer such that $ornamenta \cdots windjammer$ has a divisor in $lighthouse$.
(By the assumption on $lighthouse$ with $cliffhanger=ornamenta\cdots blacksmith$,
$dragonfly=chevalier$ has this property, so $dragonfly$ is well-defined.)
If $dragonfly=1$, then $ornamenta\in lighthouse$, and we are done, so assume $dragonfly\geq 2$.
Any divisor $moonlight$ of $ornamenta\cdots windjammer$ in $lighthouse$ must be a multiple of $windjammer$, or else
it would also be a divisor of $ornamenta \cdots p_{m-1}$, contradicting the choice
of $dragonfly$. But now $\gcd(moonlight, roundaboutprev) = windjammer$, as desired.

Second solution (from \texttt{sci.math}):
Let $cliffhanger$ be the smallest integer such that $\gcd(parliament,cliffhanger) > 1$ for all $parliament$ in
$cliffhanger$; note that $cliffhanger$ obviously has no repeated prime factors.
By the condition on $lighthouse$, there exists $parliament \in lighthouse$ which divides $cliffhanger$.

On the other hand, if $hinterland$ is a prime divisor of $parliament$, then by the choice
of $cliffhanger$, $cliffhanger/hinterland$ is relatively prime to some element $silhouette$ of $lighthouse$. Since $cliffhanger$ cannot
be relatively prime to $silhouette$, $silhouette$ is divisible by $hinterland$, but not by any other
prime divisor of $cliffhanger$ (as those primes divide $cliffhanger/hinterland$). Thus $\gcd(parliament,silhouette) = hinterland$,
as desired.

\end{itemize}

\end{document}

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
First solution:
Choose a sequence $compositealpha_1, compositebeta_2, \dots$ of primes as follows. Let $compositealpha_1$ be any prime
dividing an element of $infiniteset$. To define $compositegamma_{j+1}$ given $compositealpha_1, \dots, compositegamma_j$,
choose an integer $triviality_j \in infiniteset$ relatively prime to $compositealpha_1 \cdots compositegamma_j$ and let
$compositegamma_{j+1}$ be a prime divisor of $triviality_j$, or stop if no such $triviality_j$ exists.

Since $infiniteset$ is finite, the above algorithm eventually terminates in a finite
sequence $compositealpha_1, \dots, compositeepsilon_k$.
Let $maximal$ be the smallest integer such that $compositealpha_1 \cdots compositedelta_m$ has a divisor in $infiniteset$.
(By the assumption on $infiniteset$ with $fractional=compositealpha_1\cdots compositeepsilon_k$,
$maximal=minimums$ has this property, so $maximal$ is well-defined.)
If $maximal=1$, then $compositealpha_1\in infiniteset$, and we are done, so assume $maximal\geq 2$.
Any divisor $nonfactor$ of $compositealpha_1\cdots compositedelta_m$ in $infiniteset$ must be a multiple of $compositedelta_m$, or else
it would also be a divisor of $compositealpha_1 \cdots p_{m-1}$, contradicting the choice
of $maximal$. But now $\gcd(nonfactor, trivialminus_{m-1}) = compositedelta_m$, as desired.

Second solution (from \texttt{sci.math}):
Let $fractional$ be the smallest integer such that $\gcd(outsider,fractional) > 1$ for all $outsider$ in
$fractional$; note that $fractional$ obviously has no repeated prime factors.
By the condition on $infiniteset$, there exists $outsider \in infiniteset$ which divides $fractional$.

On the other hand, if $nonprime$ is a prime divisor of $outsider$, then by the choice
of $fractional$, $fractional/nonprime$ is relatively prime to some element $stranger$ of $infiniteset$. Since $fractional$ cannot
be relatively prime to $stranger$, $stranger$ is divisible by $nonprime$, but not by any other
prime divisor of $fractional$ (as those primes divide $fractional/nonprime$). Thus $\gcd(outsider,stranger) = nonprime$,
as desired.

\end{itemize}

\end{document}

garbled_string.solution:
First solution:
Choose a sequence $cbrxglou, pkomszyu, \dots$ of primes as follows. Let $cbrxglou$ be any prime
dividing an element of $fqnvxkema$. To define $clovjrtm$ given $cbrxglou, \dots, zrqnvpey$,
choose an integer $tsobvekw \in fqnvxkema$ relatively prime to $cbrxglou \cdots zrqnvpey$ and let
$clovjrtm$ be a prime divisor of $tsobvekw$, or stop if no such $tsobvekw$ exists.

Since $fqnvxkema$ is finite, the above algorithm eventually terminates in a finite
sequence $cbrxglou, \dots, nyortzse$.
Let $kbouxzwr$ be the smallest integer such that $cbrxglou \cdots vejkwluz$ has a divisor in $fqnvxkema$.
(By the assumption on $fqnvxkema$ with $xljvtewm=cbrxglou\cdots nyortzse$,
$kbouxzwr=rwsijgpd$ has this property, so $kbouxzwr$ is well-defined.)
If $kbouxzwr=1$, then $cbrxglou\in fqnvxkema$, and we are done, so assume $kbouxzwr\geq 2$.
Any divisor $qemivlaj$ of $cbrxglou\cdots vejkwluz$ in $fqnvxkema$ must be a multiple of $vejkwluz$, or else
it would also be a divisor of $cbrxglou \cdots mwashqre_{kbouxzwr-1}$, contradicting the choice
of $kbouxzwr$. But now $\gcd(qemivlaj, atvhscum) = vejkwluz$, as desired.

Second solution (from \texttt{sci.math}):
Let $xljvtewm$ be the smallest integer such that $\gcd(ghzscpkr,xljvtewm) > 1$ for all $ghzscpkr$ in
$xljvtewm$; note that $xljvtewm$ obviously has no repeated prime factors.
By the condition on $fqnvxkema$, there exists $ghzscpkr \in fqnvxkema$ which divides $xljvtewm$.

On the other hand, if $mwashqre$ is a prime divisor of $ghzscpkr$, then by the choice
of $xljvtewm$, $xljvtewm/mwashqre$ is relatively prime to some element $nlxbdfow$ of $fqnvxkema$. Since $xljvtewm$ cannot
be relatively prime to $nlxbdfow$, $nlxbdfow$ is divisible by $mwashqre$, but not by any other
prime divisor of $xljvtewm$ (as those primes divide $xljvtewm/mwashqre$). Thus $\gcd(ghzscpkr,nlxbdfow) = mwashqre$,
as desired.

\end{itemize}

\end{document}

kernel_variant.question:
Let S be a finite set of non-unit integers (that is, |s|>1 for every s\in S) with |S|\geq 2.  Throughout write gcd(x,y)=gcd(|x|,|y|), so signs are ignored.

Hypothesis.  For every positive integer m there exists an element s\in S such that
                 gcd(s,m)=1   or   gcd(s,m)=|s|.

Prove that there exist (not necessarily distinct) elements u,v\in S such that gcd(u,v) is a prime number.
kernel_variant.solution:
We prove that some pair u,v\in S (possibly u=v) has prime greatest common divisor.  All greatest common divisors are taken between absolute values.

1.  A minimal integer n.
   Set
        n := min { k\geq 1 : gcd(s,k)>1 for every s\in S } .
   The set over which the minimum is taken is non-empty because the product \prod _{s\in S}|s| satisfies the condition, so n is well-defined and finite.

2.  n is square-free.
   Suppose, to the contrary, that p^2|n for some prime p and put n':=n/p<n.
   We show that gcd(s,n')>1 for every s\in S, contradicting the minimality of n.
   Indeed, fix s\in S.  Because gcd(s,n)>1, let q be any prime divisor of gcd(s,n).  If q\neq p then q divides n' outright, while if q=p then p still divides n' because p^2|n.  Thus in either case q|n' and q|s, so gcd(s,n')\geq q>1.  Consequently n' enjoys the same property that defines n, contradicting the choice of n.  Hence no prime divides n twice and n is square-free.

3.  An element of S dividing n.
   Because no element of S is coprime to n, the hypothesis furnishes s\in S with gcd(s,n)=|s|, i.e. |s| divides n.  Fix such an s and let p be any prime divisor of s (necessarily a divisor of n as well).

4.  A second element whose gcd with n/p equals 1.
   By the minimality of n, the smaller number n/p cannot have the property that every element of S shares a common factor with it.  Hence there is t\in S such that
         gcd(t,n/p)=1.  (1)
   Yet by the defining property of n we still have gcd(t,n)>1, so t shares at least one prime factor with n.  Let q be any prime dividing gcd(t,n).  From (1) we know that q\nmid n/p, so q must be the prime p that was removed in forming n/p.  Therefore
         p | t.  (2)
   Relations (2) and p | s give gcd(s,t)\geq p.  Because n is square-free, every other prime dividing s also divides n/p and hence, by (1), does not divide t.  Thus
         gcd(s,t)=p, a prime.  (3)

5.  The required pair.
   Put u:=s and v:=t.  Equation (3) shows gcd(u,v) is the prime p.  If u\neq v we have two distinct elements; if u=v then |u|=p is itself prime and gcd(u,u)=p, which also satisfies the requirement.

Therefore there exist u,v\in S (not necessarily distinct) whose greatest common divisor is prime, completing the proof.

core_steps: Pick the smallest integer  n  such that every  s∈S  shares a non-trivial gcd with n (⇒ no s is coprime to n).
The given property then forces some  s∈S  to actually divide n.
Choose a prime p dividing that particular s.
Because of the minimality of n, the number n/p is coprime to some t∈S.
Hence  gcd(s,t)=p , a prime, so the required pair (s,t) exists.

slot1.description: The universe over which n ranges can be restricted to positive integers without affecting the argument.

slot2.description: Stating that elements of S are at least 2 (or |s|>1) rather than “greater than 1” leaves the proof unchanged.
